p_question-5211.ja:
いかは、留学生の作文である。


工場見学

コルホネン　アーロン

　日本に来る前に、日本には無料で工場見学ができるところがあると聞いて、面白そうだと思いました。日本に行ったら絶対に行こうと思っていたのですが、先月ついに行くことができました。

　[19]のはアイスクリームの会社の工場です。工場では、機会を使って、材料を混ぜたり型に入れたり凍らせたりしていました。工場の人の説明は丁寧で、機械や商品の説明が書かれた資料もくれたので、よくわかりました。できたばかりのアイスも食べさせてくれました。工場見学は本当に楽しかったです。[20]、どうして無料で見学をさせてくれるのかわかりませんでした。

　調べてみたら、工場見学は、会社側にもいいことがあるとわかりました。ある新聞の調査によると、工場見学をした人の大部分が、[21]で作られている商品を好きになり、会社に対するイメージもよくなったそうです。実際に、私も工場見学をした会社の印象が前よりよくなりました。

　日本には、他にも同じような工場見学ができるところがたくさんあるそうです。今、次に見学に行く工場を探しています。日本にいる間にいろいろな工場に見学に[22]。

The following is an essay by an international student.


Factory Tour

Korhonen Aaron

Before coming to Japan, I heard that there are places in Japan where you can tour factories for free, and I thought that sounded interesting. I was determined to go once I got to Japan, and last month I finally managed to go.

The place I went to for [19] was the factory of an ice cream company. At the factory they used machines to mix the ingredients, put them into molds, and freeze them. The staff’s explanations were polite, and they also gave me materials with descriptions of the machines and products, so I understood well. They even let me taste freshly made ice cream. The factory tour was truly fun. [20] however, I didn’t understand why they would let us visit for free.

When I looked into it, I learned that factory tours are also beneficial for the company side. According to a survey by a newspaper, a large majority of people who took factory tours came to like the products made [21], and their image of the company also improved. In fact, my impression of the company I toured also got better than before.

I’ve heard there are many other places in Japan where you can do similar factory tours. I’m currently looking for the next factory to visit. While I’m in Japan, I want to [22] visit various factories.

p_question-5215.ja:
（１）

　日本語学校の新学期が始まったとき、クラス全員がそれぞれ、学期の目標を紙に書いて教室にはっておくことになった。私は漢字を500字覚えることにしたが、やってみると大変だった。でも、教室に入ると、自分で書いた目標が目に入るし、クラスのみんなも知っているから、簡単にはやめられない。そのおかげで、学期末には目標の500字を覚えることができた。目標を心の中で決めるだけではなく、紙に書いて教室にはったのがよかったのだ。

(1)

When the new term started at the Japanese language school, everyone in the class was to write their term goal on paper and post it on the classroom wall. I decided to memorize 500 kanji, but when I tried, it was tough. Still, when I entered the classroom my own written goal caught my eye, and everyone in the class knew about it, so I couldn’t quit easily. Thanks to that, by the end of the term I was able to memorize the 500 characters I aimed for. It was good not only to decide the goal in my heart, but to write it on paper and put it up in the classroom.

p_question-5217.ja:
（３）

　郵便局と聞くと、まず思いつくのは手紙や荷物の配達だろう。だが、こんな意外サービスがあることを知っているだろうか。一人暮らしをしているお年寄りとその家族の安心のため、郵便局員がお年寄りの家を訪問する。そして、そのときのお年寄りの様子をEメールで家族に教えてくれるのだ。親になかなか会いに行けない人には、うれしいサービスだ。

(3)

When you hear “post office,” the first thing that comes to mind is probably delivering letters and parcels. But did you know they have this unexpected service? For the peace of mind of elderly people living alone and their families, postal workers visit the elderly person’s home. They then inform the family by email about how the person was doing at that time. It’s a welcome service for people who can’t easily go to see their parents.

p_question-5218.ja:
（４）


ナムさん

　来週の「会議の文法研究会」ですが、申し込みが予想より増えています。

　参加者は60名ぐらいになりそうですから、資料をあと20部お願いします。

　それから、今日、大学の事務所で、教室の予約を304から201に変えてもらいました。研究会の会員にメールでお知らせしておきますが、当日も304のドアに、教室が変わったことを知らせる紙をはったほうがいいと思うので、作っておいてください。

(4)


Mr. Nam,

Regarding next week’s “Conference Grammar Study Group,” the number of applications is increasing more than expected.

It looks like there will be about 60 participants, so please prepare 20 more sets of materials.

Also, today I had the university office change the classroom reservation from Room 304 to Room 201. I will notify the study group members by email, but on the day as well, I think we should put a notice on the door of Room 304 to inform people that the classroom has changed, so please make one.

p_question-5219.ja:
（１）

　今年の夏、初めて水筒を買った。今まで、飲み物はペットボトルを買っていたが、一ヶ月に五千円もかかっていたのだ。

　買ったのは、コップが付いていない、直接口をつけて飲む水筒で、飲み物の温度が長時間変わらない物だ。コップが付いている水筒もあったが、毎回コップに入れるのはめんどくさいと思ったので、①この水筒にした。

　夏は一日中冷たいものが飲めたので良かったのだが、冬になって熱い飲み物を入れたら、②困ったことが起きた。熱すぎて、直接は飲めなかったのだ。最初は1本あれば十分だと思っていたが、結局、コップ付きの水筒も買った。コップがあれば冷ますことができる。どちらの水筒もそれほど高くはなかったが、③最初に考えていたよりはお金がかかってしまった。でも、季節によって違う水筒を使うのも楽しいし、ペットボトルも買わなくなったのでこれでよかったと思っている。

(1)

This summer I bought a water bottle for the first time. Until now I had been buying drinks in plastic bottles, which was costing me as much as five thousand yen a month.

What I bought was a bottle without a cup, the kind you drink from directly, that keeps the drink at the same temperature for a long time. There were also bottles with cups, but I thought pouring into a cup every time would be a hassle, so I chose this bottle ①.

In summer it was nice to be able to drink something cold all day, but when winter came and I put a hot drink in it, a problem ② occurred. It was too hot to drink directly. At first I thought one bottle would be enough, but in the end I also bought a bottle with a cup. With a cup I can let it cool. Neither bottle was that expensive, but it ended up costing more than I originally thought ③. Still, it’s fun to use different bottles depending on the season, and I’ve stopped buying plastic bottles, so I think this was the right choice.

p_question-5222.ja:
（２）

　日本では、町の中でカラスをよく見ます。カラスという鳥は平気で人間に向かって飛んできたり、食べ物を取ったりするので、嫌がる人もいます。

　私の町では、毎日夕方になると、たくさんのカラスが、公園の木で寝るために集まってきます。声がうるさいし、数が非常に多いので怖さを感じることもあります。それで町の人たちはとても困っています。①この問題の解決方法はあるのでしょうか。

　ある町では、専門家の協力で一つの②実験に成功しました。安全な状態にいるときと危険を感じたときのカラスの鳴き方の違いを利用して、「いてほしくない場所」から「集まっても問題のない場所」にカラスを移動させることができたそうです。

　しかし、私の町には大きな森など、カラスが「集まっても問題のない場所」はありません。ですから、同じ方法は使えそうにありません。私の町に合った解決方法が見つかればいいと思います。

(2)

In Japan you often see crows in town. Crows will boldly fly toward people and snatch food, so some people dislike them.

In my town, every evening many crows gather in the park trees to sleep. They are noisy, and because there are so many, I sometimes feel afraid. The townspeople are very troubled by this. Is there a way to solve this problem ①?

In one town, with the help of experts, they succeeded in an experiment ②. By using the difference in cawing sounds between when crows feel safe and when they sense danger, they were able to move crows from “places where we don’t want them” to “places where it’s not a problem for them to gather.”

However, my town doesn’t have large forests or other “places where it’s not a problem for them to gather.” So it doesn’t seem we can use the same method. I hope we can find a solution suited to my town.

p_question-5225.ja:
私は両親結婚記念日に毎年違うプレゼントを送っていて、実家の両親も楽しみにしてくれている。ただ、もう20年以上続けているので、だんだんアイディアがなくなってきた。今年は、前にあげたことがあるものでもしかたないと、①あきらめ始めていた。

　そんなとき、友達から旅行のお土産に小さい箱に入ったおしゃれなろうそくをもらった。その夜、さっそく火をつけてみた。不規則にやさしく火がゆれて、それを見ていると気持ちが落ちついた。

　箱には、ろうそくの効果についての②説明の紙も入っていた。ろうそくの火、嫌なにおいを消す効果があることは知っていたが、料理をおいしく見せる効果があることは初めて知った。それを読んで私は、「両親へのプレゼントにいいかもしれない」と思いついた。ろうそくはあげたことがないし、これを使えば、結婚記念日の夜のごちそうがもっとおいしく感じるだろう。

　しかし、しばらく火を見つめていると、ちょっと心配になってきた。食事のあとで消し忘れることもあるかもしれない。それに、両親の家では猫を飼っているので、何が起こるかわからない。

　③ろうそくはやっぱりやめたほうがいいと思ったが、あきらめられずに一緒に入っていたカタログを見ていた。すると、実物の火そっくりにゆれて、同じような効果もある「電気のろうそく」があるのを見つけた。これなら、安全に使えそうだ。

　結婚記念日まではまだ2週間ある、このタイプには、ほかにも種類があるようなので、もう少し調べてみようと思う。

Every year for my parents’ wedding anniversary I send a different present, and my parents back home look forward to it. But since I’ve been doing it for more than 20 years, I’ve gradually run out of ideas. This year I had started to give up ① and thought it couldn’t be helped if I ended up giving something I’d given before.

At that time, a friend gave me a stylish candle as a souvenir from a trip, in a small box. That night I lit it right away. The flame swayed gently and irregularly, and watching it calmed me down.

In the box there was also a paper with an explanation ② about the candle’s effects. I knew that candlelight has the effect of getting rid of unpleasant smells, but it was the first time I learned it can make food look more delicious. Reading that, I came up with the idea that “this might be good as a present for my parents.” I’ve never given candles before, and if they use this, the anniversary dinner that night will probably taste even better.

However, after staring at the flame for a while, I started to feel a bit worried. They might forget to blow it out after the meal. Also, my parents keep a cat at home, so you never know what might happen.

Although I thought it would be better after all not to choose candles ③, I couldn’t give up and kept looking through the catalog that came with it. Then I found an “electric candle” whose light flickers just like a real flame and has similar effects. With this, it seems like they could use it safely.

There are still two weeks until the anniversary. It seems this type has other varieties as well, so I think I’ll research a bit more.

questions.stem: その会社の情報は、インターネットで見ました。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 1: じょうほう is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"その会社の情報は、インターネットで見ました。"  
→ "I saw that company's information on the internet."

**Explanation**  
The kanji "情報" is read as **じょうほう**, which means *information*. This is the standard reading and makes sense in the context of the sentence about finding information online.

**Other Options**  
2. ちょうほう – Not a standard reading of 情報.  
3. じょうぼう – Incorrect reading for 情報.  
4. ちょうぼう – Incorrect reading for 情報.

Only **じょうほう** matches the kanji **情報** in this context.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「情報(じょうほう)」 — 정보. 「会社の情報」(회사 정보)와 자연스럽게 어울린다.
오답: 2 ちょうほう는 「重宝」(요긴하게 씀)의 음으로 헷갈리기 쉬운 함정. 3 じょうぼう·4 ちょうぼう는 없는 음.
포인트: 情=ジョウ, 報=ホウ.

questions.stem: 兄は、現在は海外で働いています。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 4: げんざい is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"兄は、現在は海外で働いています。"  
→ "My older brother is currently working overseas."

**Explanation**  
The kanji "現在" is read as **げんざい**, which means *currently* or *at present*. This reading is appropriate for the context of the sentence, which talks about someone's current status.

**Other Options**  
1. けんさい – Not a standard reading of 現在.  
2. げんさい – Incorrect reading for 現在; doesn't fit the meaning in this context.  
3. けんざい – Means *healthy/vigorous* (kanji: 健在), not appropriate here.  

Only **げんざい** accurately matches the kanji **現在** and context of the sentence.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「現在(げんざい)」 — 현재. 「現在は海外で働く」(현재 해외에서 일한다)와 어울린다.
오답: 1 けんさい·2 げんさい·3 けんざい는 없는 음. 탁음(げん/ざい) 유무가 함정.
포인트: 現=ゲン, 在=ザイ. 둘 다 탁음.

questions.stem: 小さくて丸いいすがほしい。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 2: まるい is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"小さくて丸いいすがほしい。"  
→ "I want a small, round chair."

**Explanation**  
The kanji "丸い" is read as **まるい**, which means *round* or *circular*. This reading and meaning fit perfectly in the context of the sentence when describing the chair.

**Other Options**  
1. くろい – Means *black* (kanji: 黒い), and does not match 丸い.  
3. あおい – Means *blue* (kanji: 青い), and does not match 丸い.  
4. かるい – Means *light* in weight (kanji: 軽い), and does not match 丸い.

Only **まるい** matches the kanji **丸い** in this context.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「丸(まる)い」 — 둥글다. 「小さくて丸いいす」(작고 둥근 의자)와 자연스럽다.
오답: 1 くろい는 「黒い」(검다), 3 あおい는 「青い」(파랗다), 4 かるい는 「軽い」(가볍다)의 훈으로 모두 다른 한자.
포인트: 丸=まる(い). 의자 모양 묘사에 자주 쓰인다.

questions.stem: その歌は日本でも有名です。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 4: ゆうめい is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"その歌は日本でも有名です。"  
→ "That song is famous in Japan as well."

**Explanation**  
The kanji "有名" is read as **ゆうめい**, which means *famous* or *well-known*. This is the correct reading based on the kanji in the sentence.

**Other Options**  
1. ゆめ – Means *dream* (kanji: 夢), and does not match 有名.  
2. ゆめい – Not a standard reading of 有名.  
3. ゆうめ – Incomplete reading, does not match the full reading for 有名.

Only **ゆうめい** matches the kanji **有名** in this context.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「有名(ゆうめい)」 — 유명. 「日本でも有名」(일본에서도 유명)과 잘 어울린다.
오답: 1 ゆめ는 「夢」(꿈), 2 ゆめい·3 ゆうめ는 장음·받침이 빠진 잘못된 음.
포인트: 有=ユウ(장음), 名=メイ(장음). 둘 다 장음.

questions.stem: そこの通りの角に、コンビニができるそうです。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 1: かど is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"そこの通りの角に、コンビニができるそうです。"  
→ "I heard that a convenience store will be built on the corner of that street."

**Explanation**  
The kanji "角" is read as **かど**, which means *corner*. This is the correct reading for this kanji in the context of the sentence, referring to a location.

**Other Options**  
2. そば – Means *nearby* or *beside* (kanji: 側), but does not match 角.  
3. はし – Means *edge* or *end* (kanji: 端), which is not applicable here.  
4. よこ – Means *side* (kanji: 横), and doesn't fit the context of 角.

Only **かど** corresponds to the kanji **角** meaning *corner* in this situation.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「角(かど)」 — 모퉁이. 「通りの角」(길 모퉁이)와 자연스럽다.
오답: 2 そば는 「側」(곁), 3 はし는 「端」(끝/가장자리), 4 よこ는 「横」(옆)의 훈으로 모두 다른 한자.
포인트: 角=かど. 길·건물의 모서리에 쓴다.

questions.stem: 大切な物なので、丁寧に包んでください。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 4: つつんで is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"大切な物なので、丁寧に包んでください。"  
→ "Please wrap it carefully because it is an important item."

**Explanation**  
The kanji "包んで" is read as **つつんで**, which means *to wrap* or *to pack*. This reading is appropriate for the context of carefully handling an important item.

**Other Options**  
1. たたんで – Means *to fold* (kanji: 畳んで), not suitable for 包んで.  
2. はこんで – Means *to carry* (kanji: 運んで), doesn't fit the context of wrapping.  
3. すすんで – Means *to proceed* or *to advance* (kanji: 進んで), unrelated to wrapping.

Only **つつんで** appropriately fits the kanji **包んで** in this sentence.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「包(つつ)んで」 — 싸다. 「丁寧に包む」(정성껏 싸다)와 자연스럽다.
오답: 1 たたんで는 「畳む」(개다), 2 はこんで는 「運ぶ」(나르다), 3 すすんで는 「進む」(나아가다)의 훈으로 모두 다른 한자.
포인트: 包む=つつむ. 선물·물건을 포장하다.

questions.stem: それは、もう計算しました。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 3: けいさん is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"それは、もう計算しました。"  
→ "I have already calculated that."

**Explanation**  
The kanji "計算" is read as **けいさん**, which means *calculation* or *to calculate*. This is the correct reading as it fits the context of the sentence where something has been calculated.

**Other Options**  
1. けいけん – Means *experience* (kanji: 経験), does not match 計算.  
2. けけん – Not a standard reading for 計算 or any word.  
4. けさん – Not a standard reading of 計算.

Only **けいさん** matches the kanji **計算** in this context.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「計算(けいさん)」 — 계산. 「もう計算しました」(이미 계산했다)와 자연스럽다.
오답: 1 けいけん은 「経験」(경험)의 음으로 함정. 2 けけん·4 けさん은 장음이 빠진 잘못된 음.
포인트: 計=ケイ(장음), 算=サン.

questions.stem: 私は毎日、どのくらい歩いたかきろくしている。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 4: 記録 is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"私は毎日、どのくらい歩いたかきろくしている。"  
→ "I keep a record of how much I walk every day."

**Explanation**  
The word "きろく" means *record* or *document*. The correct kanji representation is **記録**, which is used for documenting or recording information such as daily activities, events, or measurements.

**Other Options**  
1. 基緑（きみどり）– The reading is "きみどり", and it means *light green*. It does not match the word "きろく".

2. 記緑（きろく）– While this has the right reading "きろく", the second kanji "緑" means *green*, making this combination incorrect for the intended meaning of "record".

3. 基録（きろく）– The reading is also "きろく", but the first kanji "基" means *foundation* or *basis*, not relevant to the word "record".

Only **記録** correctly represents the word "きろく" as used in this sentence.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「記録(きろく)」 — 기록. 「歩いた量を記録する」(걸은 양을 기록하다)와 자연스럽다.
오답: 1 基緑·2 記緑·3 基録은 「基」(기초)·「緑」(녹색) 등 비슷한 한자로 만든 함정.
포인트: 記=기록할 기, 録=기록할 록. 두 글자 모두 '기록' 의미.

questions.stem: 駅で新しいこうこくを見ました。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 4: 広告 is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"駅で新しいこうこくを見ました。"  
→ "I saw a new advertisement at the station."

**Explanation**  
The word "こうこく" means *advertisement*, and the correct kanji representation is **広告**. It is commonly used in contexts where advertisements or promotions are involved, like posters or commercials.

**Other Options**  
1. 校菅（こうかん）– The reading of this kanji could be interpreted as "こうかん", which doesn't have a standard meaning in Japanese. It doesn't match "こうこく".

2. 校告（こうこく）– This combination reads the same as "こうこく", but it doesn't convey the meaning of *advertisement*. The kanji 校 means *school*.

3. 広営（こうえい）– The reading is "こうえい", meaning something related to *broad management* or *large-scale operation*. It’s not aligned with the meaning of "こうこく".

Only **広告** correctly captures the intended meaning of "こうこく" in the provided context.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「広告(こうこく)」 — 광고. 「駅で広告を見た」(역에서 광고를 봤다)와 자연스럽다.
오답: 1 校菅·2 校告는 「校」(학교), 3 広営은 「営」(경영)으로 만든 함정.
포인트: 広=넓을 광, 告=알릴 고. 널리 알린다는 의미.

questions.stem: 体がすっかりひえてしまいました。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 1: 冷えて is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"体がすっかりひえてしまいました。"  
→ "My body has completely cooled down."

**Explanation**  
The word "ひえて" means *to become cold*, and the correct kanji representation is **冷えて**. It is typically used when referring to a cooled-down physical state or atmosphere, like in this sentence describing the body.

**Other Options**  
2. 寒えて（さむえて）– The reading of this kanji is "さむえて", and it means *to feel cold*, often used for emotional or atmospheric coldness, not physical cooling.

3. 氷えて（こおえて）– The reading is "こおえて", meaning *to freeze*, which implies a more extreme state than just cooling down, unfitting for "ひえて".

4. 低えて（ひくえて）– This is read as "ひくえて", related to *lowering* or *being low*, and is irrelevant to the context of temperature.

Only **冷えて** correctly represents the word "ひえて" in this sentence context.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「冷(ひ)えて」 — 차가워지다. 「体が冷える」(몸이 차가워지다)와 자연스럽다.
오답: 2 寒えて·3 氷えて·4 低えて는 「寒」(춥다)·「氷」(얼음)·「低」(낮다)로 만든 함정. 모두 ひえる로 읽지 않음.
포인트: 冷=찰 랭. 「冷える」(자동사)·「冷やす」(타동사).

questions.stem: けんさはしたが何も問題はなかった。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 2: 検査 is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"けんさはしたが何も問題はなかった。"  
→ "The examination was done, but there were no problems."

**Explanation**  
The word "けんさ" refers to a *check* or *inspection*, often used in contexts like medical examinations or equipment checks. The correct kanji for "けんさ" in this sentence is **検査**.

**Other Options**  
1. 健査（けんさ）– Though the reading is "けんさ," this combination is not commonly used and does not have a relevant meaning.

3. 検察（けんさつ）– The reading is "けんさつ," meaning *prosecution* or *public prosecutor's office*. This is unrelated to an examination.

4. 健察（けんさつ）– This reading is "けんさつ," but this combination is not standard and lacks a recognized meaning.

Only **検査** appropriately represents "けんさ" in this context, relating to an examination or inspection.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「検査(けんさ)」 — 검사. 「検査をする」(검사를 하다)와 자연스럽다.
오답: 1 健査·4 健察는 「健」(건강), 3 検察는 「察」(살피다)로 만든 함정. 검찰(검찰청)과 혼동 주의.
포인트: 検=조사할 검, 査=조사할 사. 건강검진·기계검사에 쓴다.

questions.stem: その話はたしかですか。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 2: 確か is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"その話はたしかですか。"  
→ "Is that story true?"

**Explanation**  
The word "たしか" means *certain* or *true*, and the correct kanji representation is **確か**. It is used when affirming the validity or certainty of information.

**Other Options**  
1. 定か（さだか）– The reading of this kanji is "さだか", and it means *definite* or *clear*. Although similar in meaning, it’s not typically used in the context of confirming the truth of a statement.

3. 必か（ひつか）– The reading is "ひつか", meaning *necessary*. This is unrelated to the context of confirming a story.

4. 常か（つねか）– This is read as "つねか", meaning *usual* or *common*, which doesn’t fit the word "たしか".

Only **確か** correctly represents the word "たしか" in this sentence.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「確(たし)か」 — 확실함. 「その話は確かですか」(그 이야기는 확실합니까)와 자연스럽다.
오답: 1 定か는 「さだか」로 읽어 음이 다름. 3 必か·4 常か는 たしか로 읽지 않는 함정.
포인트: 確=굳을 확. 「確かに」(확실히)도 함께 알아두자.

questions.stem: 私の祖父は、日本のかいがをたくさん持っている。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 3: 絵画 is correct.

**Question Translation**  
"私の祖父は、日本のかいがをたくさん持っている。"  
→ "My grandfather owns many Japanese paintings."

**Explanation**  
The word "かいが" means *painting* or *artwork*, and the correct kanji representation is **絵画**. This term is often used in the context of fine arts, specifically referring to paintings or pictures.

**Other Options**  
1. 絵面 (えづら) – The reading of this kanji is "えづら", meaning *visual appearance* or *scene*. It is not the correct representation of "かいが".

2. 図面 (ずめん) – Read as "ずめん", this means *drawing* or *diagram*. It usually refers to technical drawings, which is not related to *paintings*.

4. 図面 (ずめん) – Again, this option is identical to Answer 2 and refers to technical drawings, not suitable for "かいが".

Only **絵画** appropriately represents the term "かいが" in this context, which refers specifically to paintings.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「絵画(かいが)」 — 회화(그림). 「日本の絵画を持つ」(일본 그림을 갖다)와 자연스럽다.
오답: 1 絵面·2 図面·4 図面은 「絵」(그림)·「図」(도면)로 만든 함정. 図面(ずめん)은 설계도.
포인트: 絵=그림 회, 画=그림 화. 미술 작품에 쓴다.

questions.stem: たまねぎとにんじんは皮を（　　　）から料理に使ってください。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 1: むいて is correct. It means "peel."

**Question Translation**  
Please peel the onions and carrots before using them for cooking.

**Explanation**  
The sentence instructs someone to prepare vegetables (onions and carrots) by removing their skins before they are used in cooking. The verb むく (むいて in its て-form) means "to peel," which is the common term used for removing the outer skin of vegetables. It fits contextually as it describes an essential step in vegetable preparation.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 2: ほって means "to dig." It is associated with actions related to excavation and is unrelated to handling vegetables for cooking.
- Answer 3: 破って (やぶって) means "to tear" or "to break." While applicable to tearing paper or breaking objects, it doesn't apply to peeling vegetables.
- Answer 4: 離して (はなして) means "to separate" or "to let go." This verb implies separating distinct items rather than removing a skin or outer layer, making it unsuitable for the context of peeling vegetables.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The verb むく, when used in its て-form むいて, is commonly used in culinary contexts to indicate peeling fruits or vegetables. Recognizing commonly used verbs in cooking-related instructions is crucial for understanding and following recipes or cooking directions in Japanese.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「むいて」 — 벗기다(껍질). 「皮をむく」(껍질을 벗기다)는 정형 표현.
오답: 2 ほって는 「掘る」(파다), 3 破(やぶ)って는 찢다, 4 離(はな)して는 떼다. 모두 야채 손질과 안 맞는다.
포인트: 「皮をむく」(껍질을 벗기다) 통째로 외워두자.

questions.stem: この国には石油やガスなどの（　　　）があまりありません。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 2: 資源 is correct. It means "resources."

**Question Translation**  
This country does not have many resources like oil and gas.

**Explanation**  
The sentence discusses the scarcity of natural commodities like oil and gas. 資源 (しげん) means "resources" and is commonly used to refer to natural resources such as minerals, oil, or gas. Thus, this term fits the context of discussing the natural commodities of a country.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: 栄養 (えいよう) means "nutrition." It pertains to dietary nutrients and health, which is unrelated to the context of oil and gas resources.  
- Answer 3: 内容 (ないよう) means "content" or "substance." It is often used to describe the subject matter or content of a discussion, document, or object, making it irrelevant to natural resources.  
- Answer 4: 部品 (ぶひん) means "parts" or "components," typically used in manufacturing or mechanical contexts. It does not align with the context of discussing oil and gas resources.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The term 資源 is particularly important in discussions about economics and geography, referring specifically to the natural wealth of a country or area. Recognizing such terms helps understand broader topics related to international relations and trade.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「資源(しげん)」 — 자원. 「石油やガスなどの資源」(석유·가스 등의 자원)와 자연스럽다.
오답: 1 栄養(영양), 3 内容(내용), 4 部品(부품) — 모두 천연자원과 의미가 안 맞는다.
포인트: 資源=천연자원. 「資源が乏しい」(자원이 부족하다)도 정형.

questions.stem: 一生懸命頑張ったのに、試合に負けてしまって、とても（　　　）です。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 3: くやしかった is correct. It means "frustrated" or "regrettable."

**Question Translation**  
Although I worked very hard, I lost the match, and I am very frustrated.

**Explanation**  
The phrase 一生懸命頑張った (いっしょうけんめいがんばった) means "I tried my best" or "I worked very hard." Despite the hard work, the mention of losing the match (試合に負けてしまって) implies a feeling of regret or frustration. くやしい (悔しい) expresses the feeling of regret, frustration, or vexation over an undesired outcome, which perfectly fits the context of losing despite trying hard.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: まずしかった (貧しかった) means "was poor" or "was lacking in wealth." This does not fit the emotional context of frustration or regret after losing.
- Answer 2: あやしかった (怪しかった) means "was suspicious" or "was doubtful." This does not relate to feelings experienced after losing a match.
- Answer 4: ずるかった (ずるい) means "was dishonest" or "cunning." This term is about unfairness or deceit and doesn't apply to personal feelings of regret after a loss.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The word 悔しい (くやしい) is often used to describe feelings of frustration and regret when efforts do not lead to the desired outcome. It’s different from straightforward sadness and reflects a personal disappointment in a specific situation where one tried hard but did not succeed. Recognizing such emotional nuances is important for comprehension and appropriate expression in Japanese.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「くやしかった」 — 분하다. 「頑張ったのに負けた」(노력했는데 졌다)의 감정과 일치.
오답: 1 まずしかった(가난), 2 あやしかった(수상), 4 ずるかった(교활) — 시합에 진 감정과 안 맞는다.
포인트: 悔(くや)しい=억울·분하다. 노력 대비 안 좋은 결과의 단골 표현.

questions.stem: 旅行に行けなくなったので、ホテルの予約を（　　　）した。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 2: キャンセル is correct. It means "cancel."

**Question Translation**  
Since I could no longer go on the trip, I canceled the hotel reservation.

**Explanation**  
The context of the sentence is about not being able to go on a trip and therefore having to discontinue a hotel reservation. キャンセル (cancel) is the only option that appropriately matches the action of discontinuing or nullifying a reservation, which the sentence describes. The expression "予約をキャンセルした" is commonly used to mean "canceled a reservation."

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: ピックアップ (pick up) means "to pick up" or "collect." This doesn’t make sense in the context of discontinuing a reservation.
- Answer 3: ディスカウント (discount) refers to a reduction in price, which does not relate to canceling a reservation.
- Answer 4: チェックアウト (check out) refers to the process of leaving and settling a bill, typically at a hotel. While related to hotel stays, checking out is not the action described in the sentence.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The verb キャンセルする means "to cancel" and is commonly used to cancel things like reservations, orders, or plans. Understanding common katakana (loan) words that are often used in everyday situations, such as キャンセル, is important for comprehension and communication in modern Japanese.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「キャンセル」 — 취소. 「予約をキャンセルする」(예약을 취소하다)는 정형.
오답: 1 ピックアップ(픽업), 3 ディスカウント(할인), 4 チェックアウト(체크아웃) — 모두 예약 취소와 의미가 다르다.
포인트: 가타카나 동작 명사. 호텔·항공권 취소에 자주 쓴다.

questions.stem: コーヒーをこぼして、白いズボンに（　　　）ができてしまった。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 4: しみ is correct. It means "stain" or "blemish."

**Question Translation**  
I spilled coffee and ended up with a stain on my white pants.

**Explanation**  
The sentence describes an incident where coffee is spilled on white pants. The key word here is the result of the spill. A spill would typically result in a "stain" on fabric, which is represented by the word しみ (stain). This aligns perfectly with the context, as spilling a liquid like coffee would commonly cause a stain on clothing.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: 穴 (あな) means "hole." A hole would not be the result of spilling coffee; rather, it suggests some form of ripped or punctured fabric, which isn't indicated by a spill.
- Answer 2: 影 (かげ) means "shadow." While a shadow can be cast over clothing, it’s temporary and related to light conditions, not the result of a liquid spill.
- Answer 3: こぶ means "bump" or "lump." This doesn't fit the context since a bump or lump is a physical protrusion and not related to a stain or spill.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The term しみ in this context is commonly used when liquids discolor material, especially fabrics, indicating permanent or semi-permanent marking. Understanding words contextually related to everyday events, like spills, is essential for interpreting nuanced Japanese descriptions and scenarios.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「しみ」 — 얼룩. 「コーヒーをこぼしてしみができる」(커피를 흘려 얼룩이 생기다)는 정형.
오답: 1 穴(あな)는 구멍, 2 影(かげ)는 그림자, 3 こぶ는 혹. 옷에 커피를 쏟은 결과로 부적절.
포인트: 「しみができる」(얼룩이 생기다)·「しみを取る」(얼룩을 빼다).

questions.stem: 兄を起こすために、部屋の外からドアを強く（　　　）。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 1: たたいた is correct. It means "knocked" or "hit."

**Question Translation**  
In order to wake up my older brother, I strongly knocked on the door from outside the room.

**Explanation**  
The context of the sentence indicates someone is trying to wake up their older brother from outside the room. The action described needs to be something that would be effective in getting someone’s attention through a door. The verb たたいた (叩いた) from the verb 叩く (たたく) means "to knock" or "to hit," which is an appropriate and common method for trying to wake someone up from outside of a room. The word implies a certain force and sound that would be necessary to wake someone up.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 2: さわった means "touched." Simply touching the door would not be sufficient to wake someone up, especially from outside the room as it doesn’t create noise or disturbance.  
- Answer 3: つかまえた means "caught" or "grabbed." This action does not apply in the context of trying to wake someone up, especially since it cannot be applied to a door appropriately.  
- Answer 4: なでた means "stroked" or "patted." Stroking a door is unlikely to produce any sound or effect that would wake someone, making it unsuitable in this context.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The verb 叩く (たたく) is commonly used for knocking on doors, hitting objects lightly, or any form of tapping intended to make noise or get attention. Understanding such verbs and their appropriate contexts is crucial for correctly interpreting actions in Japanese.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「たたいた」 — 두드렸다. 「ドアをたたく」(문을 두드리다)는 정형.
오답: 2 さわった(만지다), 3 つかまえた(잡다), 4 なでた(쓰다듬다) — 형을 깨우는 강한 동작과 안 맞는다.
포인트: 「強くたたく」(세게 두드리다). 형을 깨우려 노크하는 상황.

questions.stem: 交通事故を（　　　）ために、ここに横断歩道を作るそうです。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 3: 防ぐ (ふせぐ) is correct. It means "to prevent" or "to protect against."

**Question Translation**  
It seems they will build a crosswalk here in order to prevent traffic accidents.

**Explanation**  
The sentence discusses creating a crosswalk to reduce or stop traffic accidents from occurring. The verb 防ぐ (ふせぐ) fits perfectly as it means "to prevent," which is the intended action here – the crosswalk is a preventative measure. The context of a traffic prevention measure aligns with the meaning of 防ぐ.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: 直す (なおす) means "to fix" or "to correct." This doesn't fit because the objective is not to fix past accidents but to prevent future ones.
- Answer 2: 逃す (のがす) means "to miss" (an opportunity, for example), which is unrelated to traffic safety or prevention.
- Answer 4: 守る (まもる) means "to protect" or "to defend." While somewhat related to safety, it doesn't specifically convey the sense of preventing accidents that 防ぐ does. It often means protecting something that already exists rather than preventing potential harm.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
In this context, 防ぐ is a key vocabulary word indicating proactive measures against potential negative outcomes, such as accidents. Recognizing verbs that express various forms of action (prevent, protect, etc.) and their appropriate contexts is crucial for comprehension in Japanese.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「防(ふせ)ぐ」 — 막다·예방하다. 「事故を防ぐ」(사고를 막다)는 정형.
오답: 1 直(なお)す(고치다), 2 逃(のが)す(놓치다), 4 守(まも)る(지키다) — 모두 의미가 어긋난다. 4는 비슷하지만 사고는 '예방'의 대상.
포인트: 「Nを防ぐ」(N을 예방하다). 사고·범죄·재해 등에 쓴다.

questions.stem: のどが（　　　）ので、何か飲みたいです。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 3: かわいた is correct. It means "dry."

**Question Translation**  
My throat is dry, so I want to drink something.

**Explanation**  
The sentence expresses a desire to drink something because the throat feels a certain condition. The common expression のどがかわいた (喉が渇いた, のどがかわいた) means "thirsty," literally translating to "the throat is dry." Thus, かわいた fits perfectly here as it describes the state that prompts the need to drink.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: へった derives from 減る（へる）, meaning "decreased." It's not used in reference to a throat feeling.
- Answer 2: すいた can derive from 空く（すく）, meaning "empty" or "hungry" when referring to a stomach. It's inappropriate for describing a throat.
- Answer 4: やせた derives from 痩せる（やせる）, meaning "to lose weight" or "to become slim." It does not relate to the throat or thirst.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
かわいた (乾いた) is derived from 乾く (かわく), which is an intransitive verb meaning "to dry" or "to be thirsty." Recognizing common expressions that use specific physical sensations like "thirst" helps in understanding how Japanese often describes bodily needs or conditions.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「かわいた」 — 말랐다. 「のどがかわく」(목이 마르다)는 정형.
오답: 1 へった(줄다)는 「お腹がへる」(배가 고프다), 2 すいた는 「お腹がすく」, 4 やせた(살이 빠지다)는 부적절.
포인트: 「のどがかわく」=목마르다. 「お腹がすく」=배고프다. 짝으로 외우자.

questions.stem: 行きは佐藤さんと２人で来たが、帰りは（　　　）だった。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 3: 別々 is correct. It means "separately" or "independently."

**Question Translation**  
I came with Mr. Sato on the way there, but on the way back, it was separately.

**Explanation**  
In this sentence, the context indicates a contrast between the way to somewhere and the way back. The speaker says they traveled with Mr. Sato initially, suggesting they went together. The correct choice, 別々 (べつべつ), means "separately," which fits the context of describing how they traveled back individually rather than together. This contrast between “together” and “separately” is clear and strengthens the meaning of 別々, making it the best choice to complete the sentence.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: 様々 (さまざま) means "various" or "a variety of." It describes diversity in options or types, not applicable for describing a method of traveling.
- Answer 2: 半々 (はんはん) means "half and half." It’s used to describe equal division but doesn’t make sense in this context of describing travel arrangements.
- Answer 4: 色々 (いろいろ) also means "various" or "many different kinds," similar to 様々. While it denotes variety, it doesn’t describe a method of traveling or a change from one travel mode to another.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The phrase 別々に (べつべつに) is commonly used to describe situations where actions are taken individually or separately. Understanding such expressions can help clarify contrasts in activities or states, which is essential in effectively expressing nuanced differences in Japanese.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「別々(べつべつ)」 — 따로따로. 「行きは一緒, 帰りは別々」의 대비.
오답: 1 様々(다양), 2 半々(반반), 4 色々(여러 가지) — 동행 여부의 대비에 안 맞는다.
포인트: 別々=각자 따로. 「別々に払う」(따로 계산하다)도 자주 쓴다.

questions.stem: 留学については、両親とよく（　　　）、決めるつもりだ。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 4: 話し合って is correct. It means "to discuss" or "to talk over."

**Question Translation**  
Regarding studying abroad, I plan to make a decision after thoroughly discussing it with my parents.

**Explanation**  
The context of the sentence suggests a need for dialogue or consultation before making a decision about studying abroad. 話し合って (はなしあって) means "to discuss" or "to talk over," which is appropriate because it implies having a conversation to reach a decision collaboratively. This aligns with the idea of planning to decide something important like studying abroad, where involving parents in discussion is logical.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: 取り込んで (とりこんで) means "to be absorbed in" or "to take in." This doesn't make sense in the context of making a decision with someone else, as it doesn’t suggest mutual consultation or decision-making.
- Answer 2: 言い返して (いいかえして) means "to talk back" or "to retort." It suggests a confrontational or argumentative response, which is inappropriate when intending to make a thoughtful decision with parents.
- Answer 3: 受け取って (うけとって) means "to receive" or "to accept." It doesn’t align with the context of making a decision after consideration and dialogue, as it implies passively receiving information rather than actively discussing.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
話し合う (はなしあう) is a common verb used to express the action of discussing or talking things over, especially in contexts involving mutual decision-making or resolving issues. Recognizing verbs that indicate mutual actions or processes is important for understanding Japanese sentences focused on dialogue and discussion.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「話し合(あ)って」 — 의논해서. 「両親と話し合って決める」(부모와 의논해 정하다)와 자연스럽다.
오답: 1 取り込んで(받아들이다·들이다), 2 言い返して(말대꾸하다), 3 受け取って(받다) — 의논의 의미가 없다.
포인트: 「話し合う」(서로 이야기하다·의논하다). 중대한 결정 앞에 쓴다.

questions.stem: 昨日の夜から歯が痛くて、（　　　）します。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 3: ずきずき is correct. It means "throbbing" or "pulsing," often used to describe a continuous, painful sensation.

**Question Translation**  
Since last night, my tooth has been hurting with a throbbing pain.

**Explanation**  
The sentence describes the sensation of tooth pain that persists or pulses from the night before. The word ずきずき (ずきずき) is used to depict a throbbing or pulsating type of pain, which is typical for describing toothaches. This term accurately conveys the type of discomfort experienced due to dental issues.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: とんとん is an onomatopoeia for a tapping or pattering noise, like footsteps or knocking. It doesn't describe pain or fit the context of a toothache.
- Answer 2: ざあざあ describes the sound of heavy, pouring rain. It doesn’t relate to sensations or pain and is inappropriate for describing a toothache.
- Answer 4: からから typically refers to dryness, such as a dry throat or something parched. It’s unrelated to the context of dental pain.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
Onomatopoeic expressions (擬音語 or 擬態語) like ずきずき are frequently used in Japanese to describe sounds or sensations vividly. Recognizing these words and their contexts can be crucial for understanding Japanese descriptions of physical feelings or environmental sounds.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「ずきずき」 — 욱신욱신. 「歯がずきずきする」(이가 욱신거리다)는 정형 의태어.
오답: 1 とんとん(똑똑·순조), 2 ざあざあ(비가 쏴쏴), 4 からから(바싹) — 통증의 의태어가 아니다.
포인트: ずきずき=박동성 통증. 두통·치통에 자주.

questions.stem: ふだんと同じようにやってください。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 3: いつも is correct. It means "always" or "usually."

**Question Translation**  
Please do it like you usually do.

**Explanation**  
The sentence asks someone to do something in the same manner as they normally do. The word ふだん (普段) refers to "usual" or "usual routine," so いつも (itsumo), meaning "always" or "usually," is the best fit, as it conveys the routine or habitual aspect of the action.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: みんな means "everyone" or "all." It does not convey a sense of usual or routine behavior which is needed here.
- Answer 2: 昔 (むかし) means "long ago" or "the past." It does not relate to the routine present actions the sentence indicates.
- Answer 4: 例 (れい) means "example" or "instance." It refers to a representative or model, not usual practice or routine.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The vocabulary word 普段 (ふだん) is important to recognize, as it often indicates what is normal or routine. Understanding this can help infer that いつも is the best matching word for maintaining routine behavior.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「いつも」 — 늘. 「ふだん」(평소)≒「いつも」(언제나).
오답: 1 みんな(모두), 2 昔(옛날), 4 例(예) — 평소·일상의 의미가 없다.
포인트: ふだん=普段. '평소·평상시'로 「いつも」와 호환된다.

questions.stem: あの人は短気だ。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 1: すぐ怒る is correct. It means "gets angry quickly."

**Question Translation**  
That person has a short temper.

**Explanation**  
The underlined word 短気 (たんき) means "short-tempered" or "quick to anger." This characteristic describes someone who easily and quickly gets upset or angry. The phrase すぐ怒る directly translates to "gets angry quickly," which accurately paraphrases the meaning of 短気.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 2: すぐ謝る means "apologizes quickly." This describes a behavior of being apologetic, not a quick temperament.
- Answer 3: すぐ驚く means "gets surprised quickly." While this describes someone who is easily startled, it does not convey the idea of anger associated with 短気.
- Answer 4: すぐ喜ぶ means "gets happy quickly." This refers to a positive and different emotional reaction, not the irritability implied by 短気.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The term 短気 is an adjective describing a personality that is easily irritated or gets angry quickly. It's essential to understand the emotional context associated with 短気 when identifying the correct paraphrase. Recognizing how words combine to express emotions helps in selecting the best fit in paraphrasing questions.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「すぐ怒(おこ)る」 — 금방 화낸다. 「短気(たんき)」(성격이 급함)=쉽게 화냄.
오답: 2 すぐ謝る(사과), 3 すぐ驚く(놀람), 4 すぐ喜ぶ(기뻐함) — 短気의 핵심인 '화'와 다르다.
포인트: 短気=짧을 短+기운 気. 성미가 급하다.

questions.stem: 12時にグラウンドに来てください。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 4: 運動場 (うんどうじょう) is correct. It means "sports ground" or "athletic field."

**Question Translation**  
Please come to the ground at 12 o'clock.

**Explanation**  
The term "グラウンド" is a loanword in Japanese from "ground," commonly referring to a sports ground or field where athletic activities occur. The most fitting Japanese equivalent is 運動場 (うんどうじょう), which also means "sports ground" or "athletic field," making it the correct paraphrase of "グラウンド" in this context.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: 屋上 (おくじょう) means "rooftop." This does not match "グラウンド," which refers to an open field or sports ground, not a roof.  
- Answer 2: 体育館 (たいいくかん) means "gymnasium" or "sports hall." While related to sports, it refers to an indoor facility, not an outdoor field or ground.  
- Answer 3: 公園 (こうえん) means "park." Although it is an outdoor space, "公園" usually refers to public parks, which can include recreational areas but not specifically athletic fields like "グラウンド."

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The use of loanwords in Japanese, such as "グラウンド," often indicates concepts that have been integrated from other languages into Japanese culture. Recognizing these loanwords and their closest native equivalents is crucial in understanding nuanced meanings in paraphrase types of questions.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「運動場(うんどうじょう)」 — 운동장. 「グラウンド」의 직역.
오답: 1 屋上(옥상), 2 体育館(체육관·실내), 3 公園(공원) — 운동장과 다른 장소.
포인트: グラウンド=ground. 학교 운동장 같은 야외 시설.

questions.stem: 私のおいは東京に住んでいます。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 1: 姉の息子 is correct. It means "my sister's son."

**Question Translation**  
My nephew lives in Tokyo.

**Explanation**  
In this context, the word "おい" (甥) refers to a "nephew" in English, which specifically denotes a male child of one's sibling. The term "姉の息子" translates to "my sister's son," correctly defining the familial relationship of a nephew.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 2: 姉の娘 means "my sister's daughter." This translates to "niece," not "nephew," and is thus incorrect.  
- Answer 3: 父の弟 means "my father's younger brother," which translates to "uncle." This does not describe a nephew.  
- Answer 4: 父の妹 means "my father's younger sister," also translating to "aunt." This is unrelated to the term "nephew."

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The kanji 甥 (おい) is used specifically to denote a nephew. Recognizing family-related terms is essential in Japanese, as relationships often have specific vocabulary terms that differ from English. Understanding the gender and specific family relation encoded in these terms is crucial for correct interpretation.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「姉の息子(むすこ)」 — 누나의 아들. 「おい(甥)」=조카(남).
오답: 2 姉の娘는 「めい(姪)」(여조카), 3 父の弟는 「おじ(叔父)」, 4 父の妹는 「おば(叔母)」.
포인트: おい=남조카, めい=여조카. 가족 호칭은 짝으로 외우자.

questions.stem: もっとくわしく説明してください。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 2: 細かく is correct. It means "in detail" or "thoroughly."

**Question Translation**  
Please explain in more detail.

**Explanation**  
The sentence is asking for an explanation that is more detailed. The word "くわしく" (詳しく) is an adverb meaning "in detail" or "thoroughly," which aligns with 細かく (こまかく). 細かく means doing something in detail or with precision, making it the best fit for a request for detailed explanation.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: 早く (はやく) means "quickly" or "soon." It doesn't relate to providing more detail; rather, it suggests speed.
- Answer 3: 簡単に (かんたんに) means "simply" or "easily." This is the opposite of what is being asked, as simplicity can imply lack of detail.
- Answer 4: 熱心に (ねっしんに) means "earnestly" or "intently." While it describes effort, it doesn't address the level of detail requested.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The word "くわしく" (詳しく) is important when needing more information or explanation. Recognizing adverbs that describe the manner or degree of an action is key in understanding requests or instructions in Japanese. Understanding various nuances of similar adverbs helps in choosing the correct term based on context.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「細(こま)かく」 — 자세히. 「くわしく(詳しく)」(상세히)≒「細かく」.
오답: 1 早く(빨리), 3 簡単に(간단히, 정반대), 4 熱心に(열심히) — 상세함의 뜻이 없거나 반대.
포인트: くわしく=상세히·자세히. 설명을 요청할 때 단골.

questions.stem: 諦める
opts: 今日は天気がいいから、傘を諦めて
台風が来そうなので、あしたの登山を諦める
家を出てすぐに、鍵をかけるのを諦めた
悲しい小説だったので、泣くのを諦められなかった
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 2 is correct.

**Word Translation**  
諦める（あきらめる） — to give up, to abandon

**Correct Sentence Translation**  
It seems a typhoon is coming, so I decided to give up on climbing the mountain tomorrow.

**Explanation**  
The word **諦める** means to cease pursuing an action or plan due to unfavorable conditions or after reassessing the situation. In this context, **諦める** is used naturally as it describes a decision made under the expectation of bad weather, which is a reasonable context for giving up on a plan.

**Other Options**

① 今日は天気がいいから、傘を__諦めて__よさそうだ。  
→ Since the weather is nice today, it seems fine to give up on the umbrella.  
🔶 Awkward Usage  
While **諦める** could fit in abandoning or not carrying something, more suitable expressions include "置いていく" or "要らなくなる", suggesting a conscious decision rather than giving up.

③ 家を出てすぐに、鍵をかけるのを__諦めた__ことに気づいた。  
→ Right after leaving the house, I realized I gave up on locking the door.  
❌ Incorrect Usage  
The action of forgetting or neglecting typically doesn't use **諦める**. A more appropriate word might be "忘れた" or "しなかった".

④ 悲しい小説だったので、泣くのを__諦められなかった__。  
→ Because the novel was sad, I couldn’t give up crying.  
❌ Incorrect Usage  
**諦める** is unnatural here as expressing cessation of an emotional response. A better term might be "我慢できなかった" (couldn't refrain from crying).

**Usage Note**  
"諦める" is typically applied to actions or plans involving intentional cessation due to external circumstances or reconsideration. It does not suit contexts involving forgetfulness, emotional reactions, or trivial decisions.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 — 「諦(あきら)める」(포기하다)의 올바른 용례는 「台風が来そうなので、登山を諦める」(태풍이 올 것 같아 등산을 포기한다).
오답: 1은 「持っていかない」(안 가져간다)가 자연스럽고, 3은 「忘れた」(잊다), 4는 「止められなかった」(멈출 수 없었다)가 맞다.
포인트: 諦める=계획·목표를 단념하다. 환경·상황 때문에 그만둘 때.

questions.stem: 参加
opts: 赤ちゃんが家族に参加
山川さんが事故に参加
あしたは息子と一緒にパーティーに参加
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 3 is correct.

**Word Translation**  
参加（さんか） — to participate, to join in

**Correct Sentence Translation**  
Tomorrow, I plan to participate in a party with my son.

**Explanation**  
The word **参加** is used to indicate taking part in an event, activity, or gathering. "パーティーに参加する" (participate in a party) is a natural and common expression when discussing joining events or social gatherings.

**Other Options**

① 赤ちゃんが家族に__参加__したので、生活が変わった。  
→ Since the baby joined the family, our lives have changed.  
❌ Incorrect Usage  
"参加" is inappropriate here. It implies active participation, which isn't applicable to babies joining a family. A better word is "加わった" (joined).

② 山川さんが事故に__参加__して、足をけがしたそうだ。  
→ I heard Mr. Yamakawa participated in an accident and injured his leg.  
❌ Incorrect Usage  
"参加" suggests voluntary participation, unsuitable for accidental situations. A more accurate word is "巻き込まれた" (was involved in).

④ 父は貿易会社に__参加__しているので、出張は海外が多い。  
→ My father participates in a trading company, so he often travels abroad on business.  
🔶 Awkward Usage  
"参加" implies event participation, not employment situations. "勤務している" (works at) fits better for companies.

**Usage Note**  
"参加" naturally applies to events or activities where voluntary participation is possible, such as meetings, competitions, or social gatherings. It doesn't fit contexts involving accidents, family dynamics, or employment.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 — 「参加(さんか)」(참가)의 올바른 용례는 「パーティーに参加する」(파티에 참가하다).
오답: 1 家族に参加는 「加わる」(가족이 되다), 2 事故に参加는 「遭(あ)う」(사고를 당하다), 4 会社に参加는 「入社する」(입사하다)가 맞다.
포인트: 참가의 대상은 모임·행사·경기. 가족·사고·회사에는 못 쓴다.

questions.stem: 盛ん
opts: あしたは試合なので、今日は練習時間が盛ん
このレストランは、魚も野菜も盛ん
この町は以前から外国人との交流が盛ん
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 4 is correct.

**Word Translation**  
盛ん（さかん） — thriving, prosperous, active

**Correct Sentence Translation**  
This town has long been active in exchanges with foreigners.

**Explanation**  
The word **盛ん** suggests something is thriving, widespread, or actively taking place. When describing activities like "交流" (exchange), it denotes the idea of being active, which is appropriate here. "交流が盛ん" is a natural and common expression.

**Other Options**

① あしたは試合なので、今日は練習時間が盛んだった。  
→ Tomorrow is the match, so today the practice time was thriving.  
❌ Incorrect Usage  
Here, "盛ん" awkwardly describes "練習時間." A better choice would be "長かった" (was long) or "たくさん行われた" (was conducted a lot).

② 今日は荷物が盛んなので、大きなかばんが必要だ。  
→ Today, the luggage is thriving, so I need a big bag.  
❌ Incorrect Usage  
"盛ん" is inappropriate for "荷物" (luggage); a more fitting word is "多い" (a lot) or "重い" (heavy).

③ このレストランは、魚も野菜も盛んでおいしい。  
→ This restaurant’s fish and vegetables are thriving and delicious.  
❌ Incorrect Usage  
"盛ん" is not used for describing food quality. Instead, use "新鮮" (fresh) or "豊富" (abundant).

**Usage Note**  
"盛ん" is often paired with activities, events, or conditions that are dynamic or flourishing, such as "交流" (exchange), "活動" (activity), or "ビジネス" (business), but it doesn't naturally describe time, objects, or food.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 — 「盛(さか)ん」(번성·활발)의 올바른 용례는 「外国人との交流が盛んだ」(외국인과의 교류가 활발하다).
오답: 1 練習時間は「長い」(길다), 2 荷物は「多い」(많다), 3 メニュー는「豊富(ほうふ)だ」(풍부하다)가 맞다.
포인트: 盛ん=활동·교류·산업이 활발함. 양·길이에는 못 쓴다.

questions.stem: 整理
opts: 家の廊下が汚れていたので、ぞうきんで整理
長い間使っていない部屋の窓を開けて、空気を整理
虫歯になると困るので、毎回、食事の後で歯を整理
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 1 is correct.

**Word Translation**  
整理（せいり） — to arrange, to organize, to tidy up

**Correct Sentence Translation**  
I organized the desk drawer and threw away things I didn't need.

**Explanation**  
The word **整理** refers to the act of arranging or tidying up physical spaces or items to create order or remove unnecessary things. "机の引き出しを整理する" (to organize the desk drawer) is a natural expression where **整理** means to sort and tidy the contents efficiently.

**Other Options**

② 家の廊下が汚れていたので、ぞうきんで__整理__しました。  
→ The hallway was dirty, so I tidied it up with a cloth.  
🔶 Awkward Usage  
While **整理** can mean tidying, it typically relates to organizing rather than cleaning. "掃除しました" (cleaned) would be more suitable here.

③ 長い間使っていない部屋の窓を開けて、空気を__整理__した。  
→ I opened the window of a long-unused room to organize the air.  
❌ Incorrect Usage  
"整理" is inappropriate for air; one does not organize air. A better term is "換気した" (ventilated), implying the refreshing of air.

④ 虫歯になると困るので、毎回、食事の後で歯を__整理__している。  
→ I organize my teeth after every meal to avoid cavities.  
❌ Incorrect Usage  
"整理" suggests arrangement, not oral hygiene. The accurate term is "歯を磨く" (brush my teeth), focusing on cleaning rather than organizing.

**Usage Note**  
"整理" is commonly used for organizing or tidying up objects and spaces. It doesn't fit contexts involving cleaning actions, air circulation, or hygiene, where specific verbs for cleaning or refreshing would be more appropriate.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 — 「整理(せいり)」(정리)의 올바른 용례는 「机の引き出しを整理する」(서랍을 정리하다).
오답: 2 廊下は「掃除(そうじ)」(청소), 3 空気는「入れ替える」(환기), 4 歯는「磨(みが)く」(닦다)가 맞다.
포인트: 整理=어수선한 것을 정돈·분류함. 청소(掃除)와 구별.

questions.stem: 通り過ぎる
opts: さっき店の前を自転車で通り過ぎた
バスが遅れて、待ち合わせの時間を通り過ぎて
今日はご飯の量を通り過ぎて
締め切りを通り過ぎない
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 1 is correct.

**Word Translation**  
通り過ぎる（とおりすぎる） — to pass by or to go past

**Correct Sentence Translation**  
I think it was Mr. Kimura who just passed by the store on a bicycle.

**Explanation**  
The word **通り過ぎる** naturally describes the action of physically moving past a specific location or object. In this sentence, "店の前を自転車で通り過ぎた" (passed by the store on a bicycle) is a common and appropriate use of the term, as it clearly describes someone moving past a point of reference.

**Other Options**

② バスが遅れて、待ち合わせの時間を__通り過ぎて__しまった。  
→ The bus was late, and I ended up passing by the meeting time.  
🔶 Awkward Usage  
While technically describing that the time has been "passed," **通り過ぎる** is awkward here because it usually does not refer to time. "過ぎる" is a better choice for expressing that a time has been exceeded.

③ 今日はご飯の量を__通り過ぎて__、おなかがいっぱいだ。  
→ Today, I exceeded the amount of rice, so I’m very full.  
❌ Incorrect Usage  
**通り過ぎる** does not apply to quantities of food. "食べ過ぎて" (ate too much) is correct, indicating an overconsumption.

④ 締め切りを__通り過ぎない__ように、書類は早めに出したほうがいい。  
→ To not pass the deadline, you should submit the documents early.  
❌ Incorrect Usage  
While it may seem like a logical use, "通り過ぎる" is not commonly paired with deadlines. Instead, "過ぎない" would indicate not exceeding or missing the deadline.

**Usage Note**  
"通り過ぎる" specifically refers to passing a physical object or place. It does not extend to abstract concepts like time or amounts. For those, use verbs like "過ぎる" or "超える" to convey having exceeded something.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 — 「通(とお)り過ぎる」(지나가다)의 올바른 용례는 「店の前を自転車で通り過ぎた」(가게 앞을 자전거로 지나갔다).
오답: 2·4 時間/締め切り는 「過ぎる」(시간이 지나다), 3 量는 「食べ過ぎる」(과식)가 맞다.
포인트: 通り過ぎる=장소를 통과해 지나치다. 시간·양에는 안 쓴다.

questions.stem: 友達がおいしいカレー屋があると言って連れていってくれた。友達と同じものを頼んだが、私（　　　）辛すぎた。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 3: には is correct. It means "for" or "to" in this context, indicating a comparative personal judgment.

**Question Translation**  
My friend took me to a delicious curry restaurant he mentioned. I ordered the same thing as him, but for me, it was too spicy.

**Explanation**  
In this sentence, 私には辛すぎた (watashi ni wa karasugita) uses the particle には to express a subjective opinion or reaction. This structure is used to indicate that something (the spiciness of the curry) was too much specifically for the speaker, highlighting a personal judgment or experience.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: で is used to indicate the means or method, or a location where an action takes place. It doesn't apply here as we are expressing a personal reaction.
- Answer 2: へ typically indicates direction towards a place or goal. It doesn't suit this context of expressing a personal reaction or opinion.
- Answer 4: より indicates comparison and is used when saying one thing is more or less than another. It would not work here as we're not directly comparing the spiciness of the curry to another instance.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The "には" particle pairing is often used to express subjective thoughts or opinions, indicating that the statement is specific to the speaker's perspective or experience. In this context, it highlights that the curry being "too spicy" is a personal reaction of the speaker, not necessarily a universal truth. Understanding the nuance of particles is crucial in properly interpreting Japanese sentences.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「には」 — ~에게는(주관적 판단의 기준).
오답: 1 で(수단), 2 へ(방향), 4 より(비교) — 모두 '나에게'라는 기준 의미를 못 살린다.
포인트: 「私には辛すぎた」=내겐 너무 매웠다. 감각·판단의 주체를 「には」로 표시.

questions.stem: A「（　　　）絵本、知ってる？」B「うん、知ってるよ。子供のとき、大好きだった。」
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 2: 「大きな家」って is correct. It means "the picture book 'A Big House,' do you know it?"

**Question Translation**  
A: "The picture book 'A Big House,' do you know it?"  
B: "Yes, I know it. I loved it when I was a child."

**Explanation**  
The sentence uses 「って」, which is a casual form of the quotation particle 「という」 or 「と」. This particle is often used to introduce a topic or direct quote, especially in informal spoken Japanese. In this context, 「って」 indicates that "大きな家" is the title of the picture book. Thus, the sentence is asking if they know the picture book called "A Big House."

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: 「大きな家」と - The particle 「と」 is used formally for quoting or listing items, but in this context, it's less natural and formal for introducing a title in spoken language.
- Answer 3: 「大きな家」でも - The particle 「でも」 implies "even" or "but" and is used to indicate a contrast or give an example. It doesn't fit the context of quoting a title or asking if someone knows it.
- Answer 4: 「大きな家」なんか - The particle 「なんか」 can be used to belittle or indicate something of lesser importance or relevance, which is inappropriate when asking if someone knows the title of a book.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The particle 「って」 is very commonly used in casual conversation to refer to something someone has said, indicate a topic, or introduce a quotation. It is an essential pattern to recognize in informal spoken Japanese due to its versatile use in identifying quotes, titles, or conversational topics.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「って」 — ~라는(인용·화제 제시, 회화체).
오답: 1 と는 인용은 되나 「絵本」 직접 수식이 어색, 3 でも(~라도), 4 なんか(~따위)는 의미가 다르다.
포인트: 「N1ってN2」=「N1というN2」의 회화체. 제목·이름을 들 때 쓴다.

questions.stem: 昨日見た映画は、面白いと聞いていたのに（　　　）面白くなかった
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 4: ちっとも is correct. It means "not at all" or "not in the least."

**Question Translation**  
The movie I watched yesterday was supposed to be interesting, but it wasn't interesting at all.

**Explanation**  
The sentence describes a disappointing outcome compared to expectations. The phrase "面白いと聞いていたのに" sets up an expectation of the movie being interesting. The most suitable expression to bridge this expectation with the reality of disappointment is "ちっとも," which is used in negative sentences to emphasize that something was not the case at all, thus fitting the context perfectly.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: やっと means "finally," often used to describe something that happens after a long wait or effort. It doesn’t fit the context of expressing disappointment or emphasizing the lack of something.
  
- Answer 2: すっかり means "completely" or "thoroughly," used to describe something that is thoroughly done or changed. While it emphasizes totality, it doesn’t inherently carry the negative surprise or disappointment needed here.
  
- Answer 3: せっかく means "with great effort" or "despite doing something special," usually emphasizing the wasted effort or opportunity, but it does not convey the idea of "not at all." It does signal disappointment but not in the context of the movie not being interesting.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
"ちっとも" is a negative adverb used to stress the non-occurrence of something expected or desired. It's commonly paired with verbs or adjectives in their negative forms to emphasize that something does not happen or is not true at all. Recognizing the role of adverbs like this in expressing disappointment or negation is crucial for understanding nuanced Japanese expressions.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「ちっとも」 — 전혀(부정과 호응). 「ちっとも〜ない」.
오답: 1 やっと(드디어), 2 すっかり(완전히), 3 せっかく(모처럼) — 모두 「面白くなかった」의 강한 부정과 호응이 안 된다.
포인트: ちっとも+부정=조금도/전혀 ~않다. 기대와 다른 결과 강조.

questions.stem: 近所のパン屋は、パンの種類が多い。一年中売っているパン（　　　）季節ごとに発売されるパンもある。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 1: のほかに is correct. It means "in addition to."

**Question Translation**  
The local bakery has a wide variety of bread. In addition to the bread sold all year, there are also breads that are sold seasonally.

**Explanation**  
The sentence describes two categories of bread available at the bakery: those available year-round and those available seasonally. The phrase "のほかに" is commonly used to indicate an addition to an existing item or list. In this context, it connects the year-round bread to the additional seasonal varieties, making it the most appropriate choice.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 2: のことで would translate to "about" or "regarding." This does not fit the context, as it doesn't logically connect the two types of bread discussed.
- Answer 3: に比べて means "compared to." This option implies a comparison, which doesn’t align with the idea of introducing an additional category of bread.
- Answer 4: について also means "about" or "concerning," similar to のことで, and fails to effectively link the two ideas, as the structure here isn't about explaining or discussing bread.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The phrase "のほかに" (in addition to) effectively communicates the inclusion of another item or idea alongside what has already been mentioned. It is commonly used to expand upon lists or to introduce additional details in a sentence. Understanding connectors and additional phrases is crucial in grasping the full meaning of complex Japanese sentences.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「のほかに」 — ~외에도. 「年中売っているパンのほかに、季節パンもある」(연중판매빵 외에 계절빵도 있다).
오답: 2 のことで(~의 일로), 3 に比べて(~에 비해), 4 について(~에 대해) — 추가의 의미가 없다.
포인트: Nのほかに+「Nもある」=N 외에도 ~도 있다. 추가 열거.

questions.stem: 夫は、プロのサッカー選手として活動する（　　　）大学院でスポーツ科学を学んでいる。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 2: 一方で is correct. It means "on the other hand" or "while."

**Question Translation**  
The sentence translates to: "While my husband works as a professional soccer player, he is also studying sports science in graduate school."

**Explanation**  
This sentence describes two simultaneous actions or contrasting aspects of the husband's activities. The phrase 一方で (いっぽうで) effectively connects two actions that occur concurrently but might be seen as distinct from one another, such as having a professional career and studying at the same time. It highlights a duality or contrast in roles or activities.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: 点で (てんで) means "point" or "aspect," usually used to indicate a standpoint or aspect. It doesn't fit here as it doesn't connect two concurrent actions meaningfully.
- Answer 3: としたら suggests a conditional meaning, "if," or speculative statement. The sentence is describing an actual situation, not a hypothetical one.
- Answer 4: のだから indicates a reason or consequence, meaning "because" or "since." This doesn't match the sentence structure, which is presenting two aspects of the husband's life, not explaining a cause-effect relationship.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The use of 一方で often signals parallel, contrasting, or simultaneous actions or states. It’s a useful conjunction for discussing two aspects or perspectives within the same context, which is crucial for understanding complex sentence structures in Japanese.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「一方(いっぽう)で」 — 한편으로(병행·대비).
오답: 1 点で(~점에서), 3 としたら(~라면, 가정), 4 のだから(~이니까, 이유) — 두 활동의 병행을 못 나타낸다.
포인트: 「Aする一方でBする」=A 하는 한편 B도 한다. 동시 진행·대비.

questions.stem: もうすぐ夏だ。（　　　）エアコンが問題なく動くかどうか、確認しておこうと思う。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 2: 暑くなる前に is correct. It means "before it becomes hot."

**Question Translation**  
It's almost summer. I think I'll check whether the air conditioner works properly before it gets hot.

**Explanation**  
The sentence suggests that you want to check the air conditioner's functionality in preparation for the upcoming hot weather, implying this should be done *before* it actually gets hot. "暑くなる前に" specifically means "before it becomes hot," which matches the need to complete this action prior to the arrival of the hot season.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: 暑くなるまで means "until it becomes hot." This implies you might delay checking the air conditioner until it actually becomes hot, which contrasts with the proactive intent of checking in advance.
- Answer 3: 暑くするまで means "until making it hot." This phrase doesn't make sense in this context because it suggests actively making something hot, which is irrelevant to checking an air conditioner.
- Answer 4: 暑くする前に means "before making it hot." Again, this implies an action (making something hot) that is not related to the context of ensuring an air conditioner works properly.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The construction "〜する前に" is a common grammar pattern used to indicate that an action should be completed before another action or event occurs. Recognizing this structure helps clarify that the action of checking should be done prior to the onset of hot weather.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 2번 「暑くなる前に」 — 더워지기 전에.
오답: 1 暑くなるまで는 '~까지'로 의미 어색. 3·4 「暑くする」(타동사)는 주체가 더위를 만든다는 뜻이라 부자연.
포인트: 「Vる前に」=~하기 전에. 자동사 「なる」 사용. 계절·날씨에는 자연 변화 자동사.

questions.stem: 新しいパソコンを買って1か月（　　　）使っていないのに、壊れてしまった。
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 3: ぐらいしか is correct. It means "only about" or "as little as," and is used to express a small amount with a nuance of limitation or exclusivity.

**Question Translation**  
Even though I have only used the new computer for about a month, it broke.

**Explanation**  
The sentence indicates that the speaker feels the computer broke unexpectedly early. ぐらいしか expresses that the amount of time (1 month) was surprisingly short for the computer to break. The combination of ぐらい, which means "about" or "approximately," with しか, which means "only" and implies limitation, is used when indicating that the speaker considers the amount small or not enough.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: ごろしか is incorrect because ごろ means "around" or "about," commonly used for approximating time, not for expressing a small or limited amount. Moreover, ごろ doesn't pair with しか to convey a emphasized limitation.
- Answer 2: ごろだけ is incorrect as だけ means "only," and while ごろだけ can be used to specify only a certain approximate timeframe, it lacks the nuance of limitation and expectation necessary for this sentence.
- Answer 4: ぐらいだけ is incorrect because だけ also means "only." Although ぐらい can mean "about," the combination ぐらいだけ lacks the nuance of disappointment or unexpected limitation that ぐらいしか provides.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The use of しか necessitates a negative verb form to complete the meaning of "only." Recognizing this pattern is crucial for proper sentence construction. In this sentence, the verb ending "ていない" is in the negative form, aligning with the exclusive nuance provided by しか.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「ぐらいしか」 — ~정도밖에. 「1か月ぐらいしか使っていないのに壊れた」(한 달 정도밖에 안 썼는데 고장).
오답: 1 ごろしか는 「ごろ」(시각)+「しか」 조합이 어색, 2·4 だけ는 부정문과 호응 안 됨.
포인트: ぐらい(양 어림)+しか(밖에)+부정=고작 ~정도뿐. 적은 양 강조.

questions.stem:
（薬局で）
店員 　「この薬を1日3回、食事の後に飲んでください。飲むと眠く（　　　）、飲んだ後、車の運転などはしないでください。」
opts: なってはいけないので
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 3: なることがあるので is correct. It means "there is a possibility of becoming."

**Question Translation**  
(At the pharmacy)  
Clerk: "Please take this medicine three times a day after meals. As it may make you sleepy, please do not drive a car or do similar activities after taking it."

**Explanation**  
The sentence explains a potential side effect of the medicine: becoming sleepy. The phrase なることがあるので indicates the possibility ("may" or "can") of feeling sleepy after taking the medicine, which justifies the advice against driving. This choice correctly conveys the pharmacist's warning about a potential side effect without claiming it will definitely happen.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: なればいいので (if it is good/safe) implies that becoming sleepy is desired or beneficial, which contradicts the safety advice to avoid driving.
- Answer 2: なったばかりなので (because it just became) suggests a recent change to a sleepy state, which doesn’t align with the potentiality suggested by the context.
- Answer 4: なってはいけないので (because it must not become) implies that becoming sleepy is prohibited. While not incorrect grammatically, it does not fit the context of a warning about potential effects.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The phrase ～ことがある is used to express that something happens sometimes or there is a chance of it happening. Recognizing this structure helps to understand expressions of possibility or an uncertain condition that might arise, common in safety and precautionary advice.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「なることがあるので」 — ~게 될 수 있어서(가능성).
오답: 1 なればいい(~하면 좋다, 부적절), 2 なったばかり(이제 막), 4 なってはいけない(~해선 안 된다) — 약 부작용 경고와 안 맞는다.
포인트: 「Vることがある」=~할 때가 있다(가능성). 약 설명서 단골.

questions.stem:
（インタビューで）
選手　「優勝できたのは皆さんの応援の（　　　）。ありがとうございました。」
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 4: おかげです is correct. It means "thanks to" or "because of."

**Question Translation**  
(Player at an interview)  
Player: "Winning the championship was thanks to everyone's support. Thank you very much."

**Explanation**  
The context of the sentence is an expression of gratitude. The phrase "皆さんの応援のおかげです" means "thanks to everyone's support." The word "おかげ" (お陰) is often used to acknowledge that a positive outcome happened because of someone else's help, effort, or influence. Therefore, the usage of "おかげです" is appropriate as it conveys gratitude appropriately.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: はずです means "it should be" or "it is supposed to be." This implies a sense of expectation or obligation, which doesn't fit the context of expressing gratitude.
- Answer 2: ようです suggests an appearance or impression, translated as "it seems." This is used for assumptions or observations rather than expressions of gratitude.
- Answer 3: ことです is used to state something as a fact or definition, comparable to saying "it is that." It doesn't express the causality or gratitude needed in this context.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
おかげ (お陰) is a noun used to indicate that something good happened due to someone/something's help or influence. It's often used to express gratitude by highlighting the cause of a positive result. Recognizing how to express gratitude and link outcomes to a subject is crucial in demonstrating proper Japanese politeness and nuances in communication.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「おかげです」 — ~덕분입니다. 우승 감사의 정형.
오답: 1 はずです(~할 것이다), 2 ようです(~인 듯), 3 ことです(~인 일) — 감사 표현이 아니다.
포인트: 「Nのおかげで/おかげです」=N 덕분에/덕분입니다. 인터뷰 단골.

questions.stem: （電話で）田中　「はい、営業課の田中です。」石山「会計課の石山ですが、今ちょっとよろしいですか。」田中「すみません。これから会議なんです。」田中「わかりました。では、またあとで（　　　）。」
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 1: かけ直します is correct. It means "I will call back."

**Question Translation**  
(Telephone conversation)  
Tanaka: "Yes, this is Tanaka from the Sales Department."  
Ishiyama: "This is Ishiyama from the Accounting Department. Is now a good time?"  
Tanaka: "I'm sorry. I have a meeting coming up."  
Tanaka: "Understood. Well then, I will call you back later."

**Explanation**  
The correct answer かけ直します (かけなおします) is appropriate because Tanaka needs to end the call to attend a meeting and implies he will reconnect with Ishiyama by telephone at a later time. The verb かけ直します combines かける (to call) and 直す (なおす, to redo), indicating the intention to make the phone call again.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 2: かけ出します (かけだします) means "to start calling out" or "to begin calling," which does not fit the context where Tanaka is promising to return the call later.
- Answer 3: かけています (かけています) means "is calling" or "is making a call," which implies action is occurring now, not later, and does not indicate a future call back.
- Answer 4: かけておきます (かけておきます) implies "I will make sure to have called," which suggests the act of calling is being done to prepare for something else, but it does not clearly express a return call after the meeting.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
かけ直します is a common expression used when one needs to end a call temporarily but intends to return the call. Recognizing the structure of "verb stem + 直します" is helpful as it conveys the action of 'repeating' or 'doing again.' Understanding nuances in language used in telephone etiquette in Japanese can also aid in selecting the correct phrasing in context-based questions.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「かけ直(なお)します」 — 다시 걸겠습니다. 「電話をかけ直す」(다시 전화하다)는 정형.
오답: 2 かけ出します(달려나가다), 3 かけています(걸고 있다), 4 かけておきます(걸어두다) — 다시 거는 의미가 아니다.
포인트: V+直す=다시 V하다. 「やり直す」「書き直す」도 같은 패턴.

questions.stem: （玄関で）妻「あれ、出かけるの？散歩？」夫「うん」妻「じゃあ、帰りにスーパーで卵を（　　　）？」夫「わかった。」
opts: 買っていってもらわない
買っていってくれない
買ってきてもらわない
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 4: 買ってきてくれない is correct. It means "Could you buy and bring back?"

**Question Translation**  
(Wife at the entrance)  
Wife: "Oh, are you going out? For a walk?"  
Husband: "Yeah."  
Wife: "Then, could you buy and bring back some eggs at the supermarket on your way back?"  
Husband: "Understood."

**Explanation**  
The wife is asking her husband who is going out if he could buy eggs and bring them back on his way home. In Japanese, 買ってきてくれない is used to request someone to buy and bring something back, with くれない indicating a softer, polite request often used in casual settings between family or friends.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: 買っていってもらわない means "Won’t you go and buy and (leave them for me)?" This implies buying and leaving the eggs somewhere, which doesn’t fit since the wife wants them brought back.
- Answer 2: 買っていってくれない means "Could you go and buy (and leave them)?" This also suggests buying and leaving, not bringing back. It lacks the appropriate nuance for the context.
- Answer 3: 買ってきてもらわない means "Will you buy and bring them back (for me)?" While grammatically correct, the usage with もら in casual context feels less natural between the husband and wife and tends to sound passive or dependent.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
- 買ってきて (かってきて) combines the verbs 買う (かう, to buy) and 来る (くる, to come), creating a compound phrase meaning "to buy and come (bring here)." 
- ～てくれない is a casual and polite way to ask someone to do something for you, common in informal spoken Japanese, especially within family or friends. Recognizing these nuances in verb conjugations and requests is essential in understanding the subtleties of polite Japanese communication.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 4번 「買ってきてくれない？」 — 사다 줄래?(부탁).
오답: 1·3 「もらわない」는 자기 입장의 부정으로 부탁이 안 된다. 2 「買っていって」는 가져가다(반대 방향).
포인트: 「Vてくれない？」=~해 줄래?(부탁). 「買ってくる」=사오다(말하는 쪽으로).

questions.stem: 村山「中野さん、3月で会社を辞めるって本当ですか。」中野「はい、私、4月から（　　　）。」
opts: 留学することにしたんです
留学することにしそうです
留学したことがあるんです
留学したことがありそうです
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 1: 留学することにしたんです is correct. It means "I have decided to study abroad."

**Question Translation**  
Murayama: "Mr. Nakano, is it true that you are leaving the company in March?"  
Nakano: "Yes, I've decided to study abroad starting in April."

**Explanation**  
The correct answer, 留学することにしたんです, uses the grammar pattern ことにする, which expresses a decision or resolution to do something. Nakano is explaining his decision to leave the company, making this the most appropriate choice to indicate a definite plan to study abroad starting in April.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 2: 留学することにしそうです implies uncertainty as it uses しそう, suggesting "looks like" or "seems like" I will study abroad. It doesn't fit the context where Nakano is specifying a firm decision.
- Answer 3: 留学したことがあるんです uses たことがある, indicating past experience, meaning "I have studied abroad before." It doesn't explain Nakano's upcoming plans, hence it's incorrect.
- Answer 4: 留学したことがありそうです combines past experience with probability, meaning "it seems like I have studied abroad," which is both incorrect and awkward given the context of future plans.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The grammar pattern 〜ことにする is used to express that the speaker has made a decision or choice about something. Recognizing this pattern is essential for understanding expressions of firm decisions in Japanese.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 1번 「留学することにしたんです」 — 유학하기로 했어요(결정).
오답: 2 しそうです(~할 것 같다, 추측), 3 したことがある(경험, 과거), 4 ありそうです는 시제·의미가 다르다.
포인트: 「Vることにする」=V하기로 결정하다. 본인의 결심 표현.

questions.stem:
（銀行のホームページで）
キャッシュカードの暗証番号は決して他人に（　　　）。
opts: 知られないようにしてください
知らないほうがいいでしょう
questions.expl:
**Correct Answer**  
Answer 3: 知られないようにしてください is correct. It means "please ensure that it is not known by others."

**Question Translation**  
(On the bank's homepage) "Please ensure that your PIN for the cash card is not known by others."

**Explanation**  
This sentence is instructing the user to take precautions to keep their PIN confidential. The phrase 知られないようにしてください is a request or admonition to "take measures to prevent something from happening," in this case, preventing the PIN from becoming known to others. The structure "〜ようにする" is often used to describe taking care to achieve a certain condition or result, making it apt in this context where a preventative measure is encouraged.

**Why Other Options Are Incorrect**  
- Answer 1: 知ってはいけません means "must not know." While it implies prohibition, it's less about advising precautionary measures and more about stating a rule. It doesn’t fit as the sentence is about preventing other people from knowing something, not about a definitive rule applied here.
- Answer 2: 知らせにくいです means "it's hard to inform." This expression is incorrect because the context is about preventing others from knowing the PIN, not about the difficulty in telling it to others.
- Answer 4: 知らないほうがいいでしょう means "it would be better not to know." This implies a recommendation or preference for remaining unaware, but doesn't address the need for protective action against others knowing the PIN.

**Grammar/Vocabulary Note**  
The structure "〜ないようにする" is a common way to express making sure something doesn’t happen or to take precautionary actions. This phrase is vital in Japanese when giving advice or instructions that involve taking preventative steps. It ensures that the intended result is kept in mind by the listener, which in this case, pertains to security and privacy concerns.
questions.expl_ko:
정답: 3번 「知られないようにしてください」 — 알려지지 않도록 해 주세요(수동+의뢰).
오답: 1 知ってはいけません(알아선 안 된다, 주체 혼동), 2 知らせにくい(알리기 어렵다), 4 知らないほうがいい(모르는 편이 낫다) — 모두 비밀번호 보안의 의미가 어긋난다.
포인트: 「Vられないようにする」=V당하지 않도록 하다. 수동+ないように.

questions.stem: 私は、森先生の授業を受けてから数学が好きになった____　★ ____　____いないと思う。
opts: わかりやすく教えてくれる
questions.expl:
Answer (★ Position)  
Answer 2: 先生ほど (★ Position: second)

Correct Order  
3 → 2 → 4 → 1  
📌 あの → 先生ほど★ → わかりやすく教えてくれる → 先生は

Chunk-by-Chunk Breakdown  
③ あの — demonstrative; “that,” modifies 先生 to form “that teacher.”
